nativeRange.habitat: Rheophyte growing on rocks in and along fast-flowing streams and waterfalls in perhumid lowland to hill rainforest, generally reported between roughly 100 and 900 m elevation; periodically submerged during the wet season and exposed emersed during drier periods

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape to attach the rhizome to
moderate to good flow across the rhizome to mimic its native rheophyte stream conditions
shaded or moderately lit areas; foreground to midground placement works well
stable, established tank — Piptospatha is reported to react poorly to swings in parameters

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Bucephalandra and Java fern
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge the attached rhizome
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters where the plant is more prone to melt

summary: Piptospatha ridleyi is a slow-growing epiphytic aroid from Peninsular Malaysia, kept much like Bucephalandra and Anubias — the rhizome is attached to rock or wood and never buried. New specimens frequently melt back on arrival before recovering and sending out fresh submersed-form leaves.
careNotes:
Piptospatha ridleyi is a small flowering aroid (family Araceae) from Peninsular Malaysia, recorded from Johor, Pahang and Selangor and notably the Endau-Rompin landscape, where it grows as a rheophyte — a plant specialised for life on rocks in and alongside fast-flowing forest streams and waterfalls. A note on naming: Kew's Plants of the World Online currently treats Piptospatha ridleyi as a synonym of Kiewia ridleyi, though Piptospatha ridleyi remains the name in universal use across the aquarium trade. On origin, most aquarium retailers list this species as coming from Borneo, but scientific sources place its documented range firmly in Peninsular Malaysia; Peninsular Malaysia is the origin reflected in this entry's range data.

In the aquarium it is kept much like Bucephalandra or Anubias. The creeping rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which leaves and roots emerge) is attached to lava rock, driftwood or slate using cyanoacrylate gel, sewing thread or fishing line, and left exposed to the water column. Burying the rhizome in substrate is widely reported to cause it to suffocate, rot and melt — this is one of the most common mistakes with epiphytic aroids.

Water parameters in the range of roughly 22–28 °C and pH 6.0–7.5 are broadly cited across hobby sources, with some noting tolerance up to around pH 8 in harder water. Flow is welcome but moderate flow is generally preferred over very strong currents in the aquarium, which is somewhat less intense than the species' wild rheophyte habitat would suggest. Lighting needs are flexible and on the low end: low to moderate light is preferred, and stronger light tends to encourage algae growth on the slow-growing leaves rather than benefiting the plant. CO2 injection is broadly considered optional but is widely reported to produce visibly faster and fuller growth. The plant draws nutrients from the water column rather than the substrate, so a light water-column fertilization routine is sufficient and substrate type is largely irrelevant.

Growth is slow. The species can also be grown emersed in paludariums or terrariums and will occasionally flower — producing the cup-shaped spathe characteristic of the aroid family — given high humidity.

Melt is widely reported in newly imported plants, particularly tissue-cultured and emersed-grown stock, when first introduced to a submerged aquarium environment. Existing leaves can also melt back after sudden swings in temperature, pH or TDS (total dissolved solids). As long as the rhizome remains firm and healthy, recovery is generally reported, with new submersed-form leaves emerging over the following weeks. For this reason it is generally advisable to introduce this species only into established, stable tanks. Piptospatha ridleyi is a niche specialty-trade aroid and may be sold under generic 'rare aroid' labels alongside Bucephalandra and Aridarum.
breedingNotes: Standard hobby propagation is by rhizome division: once the plant has established and put out new leaves and roots, cut the rhizome at a natural bend with clean, sharp scissors, leaving each division with several leaves and visible roots, and reattach the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape. Sexual reproduction via the genus' characteristic spathe-and-spadix inflorescence is occasionally observed in emersed and paludarium setups (and is the mechanism behind the genus' rain-splash seed dispersal in the wild) but is not a practical hobby propagation method.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Primary source (swapped from Tropica — the supplied Tropica search URL returned no product page; Tropica does not appear to stock this species). Buce Plant gives pH 6.0-7.5 (tolerates up to 8), low-to-moderate light, CO2 optional, slow growth, rhizome must not be buried, propagation by rhizome division, Borneo origin (disagrees with scientific sources — see Kew/MyBIS additional entries), 4-12 inch height range.

additional.name: AquariumPlantsFactory
additional.notes: Cross-checked Araceae family, epiphyte placement, rhizome-must-not-be-buried, low-to-moderate lighting, CO2 optional, slow growth, native to Borneo forests (per trade convention; disputed by scientific sources).

additional.name: Glass Aqua
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH 6.5-7.5, low-to-medium lighting, CO2 recommended for faster growth (still optional), slow growth, 4-12 inch height range, foreground-to-midground placement, melt-on-burial behavior, related to Bucephalandra/Anubias.

additional.name: Aquascape Room
additional.notes: Cross-checked low-to-medium lighting, CO2 recommended, slow growth, midground placement, propagation by rhizome separation, low-flow preference, smaller adult size (3-4 inches / ~8-10 cm) — the bottom of the size-range spread reflected in adultSizeCm. Also confirmed Araceae aroid relationship to Cryptocoryne and Bucephalandra.

additional.name: Plants of the World Online (Kew) / MyBIS
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy: family Araceae; Kew currently treats Piptospatha ridleyi as a synonym of Kiewia ridleyi (retained Piptospatha ridleyi here as it is the universal aquarium-trade name). Distribution per MyBIS and the Boyce & Wong Araceae monograph: Peninsular Malaysia (Johor, Pahang, Selangor), notably Endau-Rompin National Park — NOT Borneo as commonly stated by aquarium retailers. This discrepancy is documented in careNotes.
